name: Lyme Disease
signs:
Erythema migrans (bull's-eye rash)
Fever, chills, fatigue
Arthralgia and myalgia
Headache
Lyme arthritis (large joints, especially knee)
Facial palsy (early disseminated)
Heart block (Lyme carditis)
Radiculopathy
treatment:
1. Early localized: oral doxycycline x10-21 days
2. Early disseminated (carditis, neuro): IV ceftriaxone x14-28 days
3. Lyme arthritis: oral doxycycline x28 days
4. Facial palsy without meningitis: oral doxycycline
5. Prevention: tick checks, DEET, permethrin-treated clothing
meds:
Doxycycline 100mg PO BID x10-21 days (first-line)
Amoxicillin 500mg PO TID x14-21 days (pregnancy, children <8)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily x14-28 days (neuro/cardiac)
Cefuroxime axetil 500mg PO BID x14-21 days
NSAIDs for arthritis pain
ddx: Viral exanthem, Cellulitis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Reactive arthritis, Bell's palsy, MS, Fibromyalgia, Rocky Mountain spotted fever
labs:
Two-tier testing: ELISA then Western blot confirmation
CBC, CMP
ECG (heart block in carditis)
CSF analysis if CNS involvement (lymphocytic pleocytosis, intrathecal antibody)
Joint aspiration if arthritis (inflammatory fluid, PCR)
PCR of skin biopsy or synovial fluid
notes:
Ixodes tick must be attached >36-48h to transmit
Erythema migrans is pathognomonic - treat clinically without waiting for serology
Serology may be negative in first 2 weeks (seroconversion lag)
Post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome: persistent symptoms, no evidence for prolonged antibiotics
Endemic areas: Northeast and upper Midwest US
Prophylaxis: single dose doxycycline 200mg within 72h of tick removal in endemic areas
description: A tick-borne bacterial infection caused by Borrelia burgdorferi that can affect the skin, joints, heart, and nervous system if not treated early with antibiotics.

name: Herpes Zoster
signs:
Painful vesicular rash in dermatomal distribution
Prodromal pain/tingling before rash
Unilateral, does not cross midline
Grouped vesicles on erythematous base
Allodynia
Hutchinson sign (nose tip = V1, risk of eye involvement)
Ramsay Hunt syndrome (ear + facial palsy)
treatment:
1. Antivirals within 72h of rash onset (or if new lesions still forming)
2. Pain management (multimodal)
3. Ophthalmology referral if V1 involvement
4. Postherpetic neuralgia prevention and treatment
5. Vaccination (Shingrix) for prevention in adults >=50
meds:
Valacyclovir 1g PO TID x7 days (preferred)
Acyclovir 800mg PO 5x/day x7 days
Famciclovir 500mg PO TID x7 days
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (PHN)
Pregabalin 75-150mg BID (PHN)
Lidocaine 5% patch (PHN)
Capsaicin 8% patch (PHN)
Preднисone taper (debated, may reduce acute pain)
ddx: HSV, Contact dermatitis, Cellulitis, Dermatitis herpetiformis, Insect bites, Vasculitis, Drug eruption
labs:
Clinical diagnosis usually sufficient
Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells - not specific)
Direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) - VZV specific
VZV PCR (most sensitive)
Viral culture
notes:
Reactivation of latent VZV in dorsal root ganglia
PHN risk increases with age (30% of patients >60)
Disseminated zoster (>20 lesions outside primary dermatome): think immunocompromised
Shingrix vaccine: 2 doses, >90% effective, recommended age >=50 or immunocompromised >=19
HZO (herpes zoster ophthalmicus): V1 dermatome, can cause keratitis, uveitis, vision loss
Contagious to those without varicella immunity (causes chickenpox, not zoster)
description: A painful blistering skin rash caused by reactivation of the chickenpox virus (varicella-zoster) in a nerve, typically occurring in a single strip on one side of the body.

name: COVID-19
signs:
Fever and cough
Dyspnea
Fatigue
Myalgia
Anosmia/ageusia
Sore throat
Diarrhea
Hypoxemia (often silent)
Ground-glass opacities on CT
treatment:
1. Mild: supportive care, monitor for progression
2. Moderate-severe: remdesivir, dexamethasone if hypoxic
3. Critical: mechanical ventilation, prone positioning
4. Early outpatient: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) within 5 days
5. Anticoagulation for hospitalized patients
6. Vaccination for prevention
meds:
Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) 300/100mg BID x5 days (outpatient, high-risk)
Dexamethasone 6mg daily x10 days (if requiring O2)
Remdesivir 200mg IV day 1, then 100mg daily x5 days
Baricitinib 4mg daily (with remdesivir or dexamethasone)
Tocilizumab 8mg/kg IV x1 (progressive disease)
Enoxaparin prophylactic dosing
ddx: Influenza, RSV, Bacterial pneumonia, PE, CHF exacerbation, Other viral pneumonia
labs:
SARS-CoV-2 PCR (nasopharyngeal swab)
Rapid antigen test
CXR (bilateral infiltrates)
CT chest (ground-glass opacities, crazy-paving)
CBC (lymphopenia)
D-dimer, ferritin, CRP, LDH (prognostic markers)
ProCalcitonin (bacterial co-infection)
notes:
Risk factors for severe disease: age >65, obesity, diabetes, heart disease, immunocompromised
Cytokine storm: marked inflammatory response, often day 7-10
Paxlovid interactions: check CYP3A4 drug interactions (ritonavir component)
Long COVID: persistent symptoms >4 weeks post-infection
Vaccination remains most effective preventive measure
Viral rebound possible after Paxlovid treatment
description: A respiratory illness caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus, ranging from mild cold-like symptoms to severe pneumonia and multi-organ failure.

name: Influenza
signs:
Sudden onset fever and chills
Myalgia and body aches
Headache
Dry cough
Sore throat
Fatigue
Rhinorrhea
Nausea/vomiting (more common in children)
treatment:
1. Antivirals within 48h of symptom onset
2. Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics)
3. Hospitalize if severe or high-risk
4. Annual vaccination for prevention
5. Monitor for secondary bacterial pneumonia
meds:
Oseltamivir 75mg PO BID x5 days
Baloxavir marboxil 40-80mg PO x1 dose
Peramivir 600mg IV x1 dose
Acetaminophen 1g q6h or Ibuprofen 400mg q6h PRN
Avoid aspirin in children (Reye syndrome)
ddx: COVID-19, RSV, Common cold, Bacterial pneumonia, Strep pharyngitis, Mononucleosis
labs:
Rapid influenza diagnostic test (RIDT)
Influenza PCR (most sensitive)
CXR if pneumonia suspected
CBC
Metabolic panel
ProCalcitonin (distinguish viral vs bacterial)
notes:
Types A (pandemic potential) and B (seasonal)
High-risk groups: age >65, <5, pregnant, immunocompromised, chronic diseases
Complications: pneumonia (primary viral or secondary bacterial), myocarditis, encephalitis, rhabdomyolysis
Treat within 48h for best benefit, but still treat hospitalized patients regardless of timing
Annual vaccination: reformulated each year for circulating strains
description: A contagious respiratory viral infection causing fever, body aches, and respiratory symptoms, with potential for serious complications especially in high-risk populations.

name: Toxic Shock Syndrome
signs:
High fever (>=102F)
Diffuse macular erythroderma (sunburn-like rash)
Hypotension (SBP <90)
Desquamation 1-2 weeks later (palms and soles)
Multi-organ involvement: vomiting/diarrhea, myalgia, confusion, renal failure
Strawberry tongue
Conjunctival hyperemia
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
2. Remove source (tampon, wound packing)
3. IV antibiotics: clindamycin + vancomycin or nafcillin
4. Vasopressors if refractory hypotension
5. IVIG for refractory cases
6. Surgical debridement of wound if source
meds:
Clindamycin 900mg IV q8h (inhibits toxin production)
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (if MSSA confirmed)
Normal saline boluses (often need 10-20L/day)
Norepinephrine if vasopressors needed
IVIG 1g/kg day 1, then 0.5g/kg days 2-3
ddx: Meningococcemia, Necrotizing fasciitis, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, Drug reaction (DRESS, SJS), Kawasaki disease, Scarlet fever, Rocky Mountain spotted fever
labs:
Blood cultures (often negative in staph TSS)
Wound cultures
CBC (leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia)
BMP (elevated creatinine)
CPK (elevated)
LFTs (elevated bilirubin, transaminases)
Coagulation studies
Urinalysis (sterile pyuria)
notes:
Staphylococcal TSS: S. aureus TSST-1 toxin, associated with tampon use, wound packing, nasal packing
Streptococcal TSS: Group A Strep, usually from soft tissue infection, higher mortality
Clindamycin is critical: suppresses toxin production at ribosomal level
Staph TSS diagnostic criteria: fever, rash, hypotension, >=3 organ systems, desquamation
Recurrence risk 30-40% in menstrual TSS - advise against tampon use
description: A rare, life-threatening condition caused by bacterial toxins (usually Staphylococcus or Streptococcus) that triggers a massive inflammatory response with fever, rash, and dangerously low blood pressure.

name: Syphilis
signs:
Primary: painless chancre (firm, round ulcer)
Secondary: diffuse maculopapular rash (including palms/soles), condylomata lata, mucous patches, lymphadenopathy
Latent: asymptomatic
Tertiary: gummas, aortitis, tabes dorsalis
Neurosyphilis: headache, confusion, Argyll Robertson pupils
treatment:
1. Primary/secondary/early latent (<1yr): single IM benzathine penicillin G
2. Late latent/unknown duration: 3 weekly IM benzathine penicillin G doses
3. Neurosyphilis: IV aqueous penicillin G x10-14 days
4. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction: NSAIDs (warn patient)
5. Screen and treat sexual partners
6. Test for HIV (co-infection common)
meds:
Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM x1 (primary/secondary)
Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM weekly x3 (late latent/tertiary)
Aqueous penicillin G 3-4 million units IV q4h x10-14 days (neurosyphilis)
Doxycycline 100mg PO BID x14-28 days (penicillin allergy, non-pregnant)
Ceftriaxone 1-2g IM/IV daily x10-14 days (alternative)
ddx: HSV, Chancroid, Lymphogranuloma venereum, Drug eruption, Pityriasis rosea, Psoriasis, HIV acute retroviral syndrome
labs:
Non-treponemal: RPR or VDRL (screening, follow quantitative titers)
Treponemal: FTA-ABS or TP-PA (confirmatory, remains positive for life)
Reverse sequence screening: treponemal test first, then non-treponemal
Darkfield microscopy (chancre)
CSF analysis if neurosyphilis suspected (VDRL, protein, cell count)
HIV testing
notes:
Painless chancre distinguishes from herpes (painful) and chancroid (painful, purulent)
Secondary syphilis rash on palms and soles is classic - few diseases do this (also RMSF, endocarditis)
Jarisch-Herxheimer: fever, headache, myalgia within 24h of treatment (spirochete lysis)
Argyll Robertson pupils: accommodate but don't react to light (prostitute's pupil)
Congenital syphilis: screen all pregnant women, treat to prevent transmission
4-fold decline in RPR titer = adequate treatment response
description: A sexually transmitted bacterial infection caused by Treponema pallidum that progresses through distinct stages, from painless sores to widespread rash to potentially serious heart and brain complications if untreated.

name: Pneumocystis Pneumonia
signs:
Progressive dyspnea over days to weeks
Dry nonproductive cough
Fever
Hypoxemia (especially with exertion)
Tachypnea
Clear lung exam initially
Oral thrush often present
treatment:
1. TMP-SMX is first-line treatment
2. Adjunctive corticosteroids if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient >35
3. Alternative agents if sulfa allergy
4. Supplemental oxygen
5. Prophylaxis when CD4 <200 in HIV patients
meds:
TMP-SMX 15-20mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV/PO divided TID x21 days
Prednisone 40mg BID x5d, then 40mg daily x5d, then 20mg daily x11d (if hypoxic)
Pentamidine 4mg/kg IV daily (alternative)
Atovaquone 750mg PO BID (mild disease alternative)
Dapsone 100mg + TMP 15mg/kg daily
Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h + primaquine 30mg daily
ddx: Bacterial pneumonia, TB, CMV pneumonitis, Kaposi sarcoma, Lymphoma, Fungal pneumonia, COVID-19
labs:
CXR (bilateral diffuse interstitial/GGO infiltrates)
CT chest (ground-glass opacities)
ABG (hypoxemia, elevated A-a gradient)
LDH elevated (>500 suggests PJP)
Induced sputum or BAL with DFA or PCR for Pneumocystis
CD4 count
Beta-glucan (elevated)
HIV viral load
notes:
Classic presentation in HIV with CD4 <200
Subacute onset over weeks (unlike bacterial pneumonia)
LDH correlates with disease severity and prognosis
Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX DS daily or dapsone 100mg daily when CD4 <200
Non-HIV immunocompromised: more acute/fulminant presentation
Pneumocystis jirovecii (previously called P. carinii - name changed but PCP abbreviation kept)
description: A serious fungal lung infection caused by Pneumocystis jirovecii that primarily affects people with weakened immune systems, especially those with HIV/AIDS and very low CD4 counts.

name: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
signs:
Fever
Headache (severe)
Rash: starts on wrists/ankles then spreads centrally (including palms/soles)
Myalgia
Nausea/vomiting
Abdominal pain
Confusion
Petechiae (late, indicates severe disease)
Edema
treatment:
1. Start doxycycline immediately based on clinical suspicion
2. Do NOT wait for confirmatory testing
3. Supportive care: IV fluids, monitor for complications
4. ICU admission for severe cases
5. Doxycycline is drug of choice for ALL ages including children
meds:
Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV BID until afebrile x3 days (minimum 5-7 days)
Doxycycline in children: 2.2mg/kg BID (do NOT withhold due to age)
Chloramphenicol (only if true doxycycline allergy - inferior outcomes)
ddx: Meningococcemia, Ehrlichiosis, Lyme disease, Measles, Drug eruption, Toxic shock syndrome, Endocarditis, Secondary syphilis
labs:
Clinical diagnosis - treat empirically (don't wait for results)
Rickettsia rickettsii IgG/IgM (acute and convalescent, 2-3 week delay)
Skin biopsy with immunohistochemistry
CBC (thrombocytopenia, leukopenia or leukocytosis)
BMP (hyponatremia, elevated creatinine)
LFTs (elevated transaminases)
Coagulation studies (DIC possible)
notes:
Most lethal tick-borne illness in US - mortality 20-25% without treatment
Classic triad: fever, headache, rash (but rash absent in 10-15% early on)
Rash centripetal spread: ankles/wrists → trunk (opposite of most exanthems)
Do NOT delay treatment waiting for serology - high mortality with delayed treatment
Doxycycline safe in children for short courses (AAP recommends regardless of age)
Dermacentor tick (American dog tick, Rocky Mountain wood tick) is vector
description: A potentially fatal tick-borne bacterial infection caused by Rickettsia rickettsii that causes fever, severe headache, and a characteristic rash spreading from the extremities inward.

name: Osteomyelitis
signs:
Localized bone pain and tenderness
Fever
Swelling and warmth over affected area
Reduced range of motion
Draining sinus tract (chronic)
Refusal to bear weight (children)
Open fracture or recent surgery (risk factor)
treatment:
1. Obtain bone culture before starting antibiotics if possible
2. Prolonged IV antibiotics (4-6 weeks)
3. Surgical debridement for chronic osteomyelitis or abscess
4. Remove infected hardware if present
5. Wound care and vascular assessment (diabetic foot)
6. Hyperbaric oxygen (adjunctive, limited evidence)
meds:
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (MSSA)
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (MRSA)
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (gram-negative coverage)
Ciprofloxacin 750mg PO BID + rifampin 300mg PO BID (oral step-down)
Linezolid 600mg PO BID (MRSA oral option)
Metronidazole 500mg PO TID (anaerobes, diabetic foot)
ddx: Cellulitis, Septic arthritis, Gout, Fracture, Bone tumor (Ewing sarcoma, osteosarcoma), Charcot arthropathy, Avascular necrosis
labs:
MRI (most sensitive and specific imaging)
Bone biopsy with culture (gold standard)
Blood cultures
CBC, ESR, CRP (ESR most useful for monitoring)
Plain radiographs (changes take 10-14 days to appear)
Bone scan (if MRI contraindicated)
Probe-to-bone test (diabetic foot ulcers - positive = likely osteo)
notes:
Hematogenous: children (metaphysis of long bones), adults (vertebral bodies)
Contiguous: diabetic foot ulcers, decubitus ulcers, post-surgical
S. aureus most common organism overall
Vertebral osteomyelitis: S. aureus, TB (Pott disease), gram-negatives
Diabetic foot: polymicrobial (S. aureus, strep, gram-negatives, anaerobes)
Probe-to-bone positive predictive value ~89% in diabetic foot ulcers
description: A bone infection usually caused by bacteria reaching the bone through the bloodstream, an open wound, or spread from nearby infected tissue, requiring prolonged antibiotic treatment.

name: Infectious Mononucleosis
signs:
Fever
Severe pharyngitis with tonsillar exudates
Cervical lymphadenopathy (posterior chain)
Fatigue (profound, lasting weeks)
Splenomegaly
Hepatomegaly
Palatal petechiae
Maculopapular rash (especially after amoxicillin)
treatment:
1. Supportive care: rest, hydration, analgesics
2. Avoid contact sports for 3-4 weeks (splenic rupture risk)
3. Corticosteroids for airway compromise or severe cases
4. Return to activity guided by resolution of splenomegaly
5. Avoid amoxicillin/ampicillin (causes rash)
meds:
Acetaminophen 1g q6h PRN
Ibuprofen 400mg q6h PRN
Prednisone 40-60mg daily taper (severe airway compromise only)
Avoid: amoxicillin, ampicillin (causes morbilliform rash in 70-100%)
No antiviral therapy is effective
ddx: Strep pharyngitis, CMV mononucleosis, Acute HIV, Toxoplasmosis, Lymphoma, Hepatitis, Adenovirus
labs:
Heterophile antibody test (Monospot) - may be negative first week
EBV-specific antibodies: VCA IgM (acute), VCA IgG (past/current), EBNA (past)
CBC with differential: atypical lymphocytes (>10%)
LFTs (elevated transaminases in 80%)
Peripheral smear (Downey cells)
Abdominal US if splenomegaly concern
notes:
EBV causes 90% of mono; CMV accounts for most heterophile-negative cases
Monospot: sensitivity 85% in adults, poor in children <4 and first week
Splenic rupture: rare (0.1-0.5%) but potentially fatal, avoid contact sports 3-4 weeks
Amoxicillin rash: NOT a true penicillin allergy (temporary EBV-mediated)
Complications: splenic rupture, airway obstruction, hepatitis, hemolytic anemia, Guillain-Barré
Prolonged fatigue (weeks to months) is common and does not indicate chronic EBV
description: A common viral infection caused by Epstein-Barr virus, typically affecting teenagers and young adults, causing severe sore throat, swollen lymph nodes, and prolonged fatigue.

name: Hypothyroidism
signs:
Fatigue
Weight gain
Cold intolerance
Constipation
Dry skin and hair
Bradycardia
Delayed relaxation of deep tendon reflexes
Periorbital and peripheral edema
Hoarseness
Depression
Menstrual irregularities
treatment:
1. Levothyroxine replacement (first-line)
2. Start low and titrate in elderly/cardiac patients
3. Take on empty stomach 30-60min before breakfast
4. Monitor TSH q6-8 weeks until stable, then annually
5. Myxedema coma: IV levothyroxine + hydrocortisone
meds:
Levothyroxine 1.6mcg/kg/day (full replacement dose)
Starting dose: 25-50mcg daily (elderly or cardiac)
Liothyronine (T3) 5-25mcg daily (rarely needed, combination therapy debated)
Myxedema coma: levothyroxine 200-400mcg IV then 50-100mcg daily + hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h
ddx: Depression, Iron deficiency anemia, Chronic fatigue syndrome, Sleep apnea, CHF, Nephrotic syndrome, Medication side effects
labs:
TSH elevated (primary hypothyroidism)
Free T4 decreased
TPO antibodies (Hashimoto's thyroiditis)
CBC (macrocytic anemia)
Lipid panel (hypercholesterolemia)
BMP (hyponatremia)
CPK (may be elevated)
notes:
Hashimoto thyroiditis: most common cause in iodine-sufficient areas
Subclinical hypothyroidism: elevated TSH, normal free T4 - treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic
Pregnancy: increased levothyroxine requirement (~30% increase), target TSH <2.5 first trimester
Myxedema coma: hypothermia, AMS, bradycardia - medical emergency, give stress-dose steroids before T4
Drug interactions: calcium, iron, PPI decrease absorption (separate by 4h)
Biotin supplements can interfere with thyroid lab assays
description: A condition where the thyroid gland doesn't produce enough thyroid hormone, slowing down the body's metabolism and causing fatigue, weight gain, and cold sensitivity.

name: Hyperthyroidism
signs:
Weight loss despite increased appetite
Heat intolerance and sweating
Tachycardia and palpitations
Tremor (fine)
Anxiety and irritability
Exophthalmos (Graves disease)
Pretibial myxedema (Graves)
Diffuse goiter
Diarrhea
Menstrual irregularities
treatment:
1. Beta-blocker for symptom control
2. Antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred, PTU in first trimester)
3. Radioactive iodine ablation (definitive)
4. Thyroidectomy (large goiter, pregnancy, ophthalmopathy)
5. Thyroid storm: PTU, iodine, propranolol, hydrocortisone, cooling
meds:
Methimazole 10-30mg daily (first-line)
Propylthiouracil 100-150mg TID (first trimester pregnancy only)
Propranolol 20-40mg TID-QID
Atenolol 25-100mg daily
Lugol solution or SSKI (thyroid storm, preoperative)
Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h (thyroid storm)
ddx: Anxiety disorder, Pheochromocytoma, Factitious thyrotoxicosis, Toxic adenoma, Subacute thyroiditis, Drug-induced (amiodarone, lithium)
labs:
TSH suppressed (<0.1)
Free T4 and T3 elevated
TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb) - Graves disease
Thyroid uptake and scan (diffuse uptake = Graves, focal = toxic adenoma, low = thyroiditis)
CBC before starting thionamides (baseline)
LFTs
notes:
Graves disease: most common cause, autoimmune (TRAb stimulates TSH receptor)
Methimazole: risk of agranulocytosis (0.3%) - monitor CBC, warn about sore throat/fever
PTU: hepatotoxicity risk, only use in first trimester and thyroid storm
Radioactive iodine: most patients become hypothyroid (expected outcome)
Graves ophthalmopathy: may worsen with RAI - give steroids, consider surgery instead
Subclinical hyperthyroidism: suppressed TSH, normal FT4/T3 - monitor vs treat based on age/risk
description: A condition where the thyroid gland produces too much thyroid hormone, speeding up the body's metabolism and causing weight loss, rapid heartbeat, and nervousness.

name: Diabetes Mellitus Type 2
signs:
Often asymptomatic initially
Polyuria and polydipsia
Fatigue
Blurred vision
Slow-healing wounds
Recurrent infections
Acanthosis nigricans
Peripheral neuropathy
Obesity (common association)
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modification: diet, exercise, weight loss
2. Metformin (first-line pharmacotherapy)
3. Add SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA if cardiovascular/renal disease
4. Individualize HbA1c target (usually <7%)
5. Screen for complications annually: eyes, feet, kidneys
6. Cardiovascular risk reduction: statin, BP control
meds:
Metformin 500mg BID titrate to 1000mg BID
Empagliflozin 10-25mg daily (SGLT2i)
Semaglutide 0.25-1mg SC weekly (GLP-1 RA)
Insulin glargine 10u SC at bedtime (titrate)
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily
Atorvastatin 40mg daily
Aspirin 81mg daily (if cardiovascular risk)
ddx: Type 1 diabetes, LADA, Steroid-induced diabetes, Cushing syndrome, Acromegaly, Pheochromocytoma, Pancreatic disease
labs:
HbA1c >=6.5% (or FPG >=126, or 2h OGTT >=200, or random glucose >=200 with symptoms)
Fasting lipid panel
BMP (creatinine, eGFR)
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR)
HbA1c q3-6 months
Dilated eye exam annually
Foot exam annually
LFTs
notes:
ADA screening: all adults >=35, or BMI >=25 with risk factors
Cardiovascular benefit: SGLT2i (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) and GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide)
Renal benefit: SGLT2i and finerenone
Hypoglycemia risk: insulin, sulfonylureas (avoid in elderly)
Metformin: hold if eGFR <30, hold before iodinated contrast
Sick day rules: hold metformin, SGLT2i during acute illness
description: A chronic metabolic disorder where the body becomes resistant to insulin or doesn't produce enough, leading to elevated blood sugar levels and long-term complications affecting the eyes, kidneys, nerves, and heart.

name: Cushing's Syndrome
signs:
Central obesity with thin extremities
Moon facies
Dorsocervical fat pad (buffalo hump)
Wide purple striae
Easy bruising
Proximal muscle weakness
Hirsutism
Hypertension
Hyperglycemia
Osteoporosis
Mood changes
treatment:
1. Identify and treat cause (iatrogenic most common - taper steroids)
2. Pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease): transsphenoidal surgery
3. Adrenal adenoma/carcinoma: adrenalectomy
4. Ectopic ACTH: treat underlying tumor
5. Medical therapy while awaiting surgery or if inoperable
6. Bilateral adrenalectomy (last resort)
meds:
Ketoconazole 200-400mg BID (steroidogenesis inhibitor)
Metyrapone 250-750mg TID
Mifepristone 300-1200mg daily (glucocorticoid receptor antagonist)
Osilodrostat 2-7mg BID
Pasireotide 0.6-0.9mg SC BID (pituitary)
Cabergoline 1-3mg weekly (pituitary)
ddx: Metabolic syndrome, PCOS, Alcoholism (pseudo-Cushing), Depression, Exogenous glucocorticoid use, Simple obesity
labs:
24h urine free cortisol (elevated)
Late-night salivary cortisol (elevated)
1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol >1.8 = positive)
ACTH level: low = adrenal, high = pituitary or ectopic
High-dose dex suppression: suppresses = pituitary (Cushing disease)
Pituitary MRI
CT chest/abdomen (ectopic ACTH source)
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (distinguishes pituitary from ectopic)
notes:
Exogenous steroids are #1 cause (iatrogenic) - always ask about steroid use
Cushing disease = pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma (most common endogenous cause, 70%)
Ectopic ACTH: small cell lung cancer, carcinoid, medullary thyroid cancer
Nelson syndrome: aggressive pituitary adenoma growth after bilateral adrenalectomy
>=2 positive screening tests needed before further workup
Pseudo-Cushing: alcoholism, depression, obesity - distinguished by CRH stimulation test
description: A hormonal disorder caused by prolonged exposure to high levels of cortisol, either from the body's overproduction or from taking corticosteroid medications, causing characteristic weight gain and metabolic changes.

name: Chronic Kidney Disease
signs:
Often asymptomatic until advanced
Fatigue
Edema (periorbital, peripheral)
Hypertension
Nausea/anorexia (uremia)
Pruritus
Restless legs
Nocturia
Foamy urine (proteinuria)
Metallic taste
Pericardial friction rub (uremic pericarditis)
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause (diabetes, hypertension)
2. ACEi/ARB for proteinuria and BP control
3. SGLT2 inhibitor (CKD with proteinuria)
4. Sodium and potassium restriction
5. Phosphate binders and vitamin D
6. EPO for anemia (Hgb <10)
7. Dialysis or transplant for ESRD
8. Avoid nephrotoxins
meds:
Lisinopril 5-40mg daily (proteinuria reduction)
Dapagliflozin 10mg daily (slows CKD progression)
Finerenone 10-20mg daily (diabetic CKD)
Sevelamer 800mg TID with meals (phosphate binder)
Calcitriol 0.25mcg daily
Epoetin alfa 50-300 units/kg SC 3x/week
Sodium bicarbonate 650mg TID (metabolic acidosis)
Furosemide 40-160mg daily (volume overload)
ddx: Acute kidney injury, Prerenal azotemia (dehydration), Nephrotic syndrome, Urinary obstruction, Renal artery stenosis
labs:
BMP (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarb)
eGFR (staging: G1 >90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15)
UACR (albumin-to-creatinine ratio)
CBC (anemia)
Calcium, phosphorus, PTH, vitamin D
Renal ultrasound
Lipid panel
HbA1c (diabetics)
Hep B/C, HIV (pre-transplant)
notes:
Leading causes: diabetes (40%), hypertension (25%), glomerulonephritis (10%)
ACEi/ARB: may increase creatinine up to 30% initially (acceptable, don't stop)
SGLT2i: may also cause initial eGFR dip (hemodynamic, not harmful)
Refer to nephrology at eGFR <30 or rapidly declining
AVF (arteriovenous fistula) creation when eGFR <20 (needs 2-3 months to mature)
Avoid: NSAIDs, contrast dye (if possible), aminoglycosides, excessive potassium
description: A gradual loss of kidney function over months to years, most commonly caused by diabetes and high blood pressure, that can progress to kidney failure requiring dialysis or transplantation.

name: Glomerulonephritis
signs:
Hematuria (cola/tea-colored urine)
Proteinuria
Hypertension
Edema (periorbital, lower extremity)
Oliguria
Flank pain
Fever (post-infectious)
Skin rash or joint pain (systemic disease)
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause
2. Blood pressure control (ACEi/ARB preferred)
3. Immunosuppression for autoimmune causes
4. Sodium and fluid restriction
5. Diuretics for edema
6. Dialysis if severe AKI
7. Plasmapheresis for anti-GBM, ANCA vasculitis
meds:
Lisinopril 5-40mg daily
Furosemide 40-80mg IV/PO
Methylprednisolone 1g IV x3 days then prednisone taper
Cyclophosphamide 2mg/kg daily or IV pulse (ANCA vasculitis, anti-GBM)
Rituximab 375mg/m2 weekly x4 (ANCA vasculitis)
Mycophenolate 1-1.5g BID (lupus nephritis)
ddx: IgA nephropathy, Post-streptococcal GN, Lupus nephritis, ANCA-associated vasculitis, Anti-GBM disease, Membranoproliferative GN, Alport syndrome
labs:
Urinalysis (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts = pathognomonic)
Urine protein quantification
BMP (elevated creatinine)
Complement levels (C3, C4)
ANA, anti-dsDNA (lupus)
c-ANCA/p-ANCA
Anti-GBM antibodies
ASO titer (post-strep)
Hepatitis B/C serologies
Renal biopsy (definitive diagnosis)
Renal ultrasound
notes:
Nephritic syndrome: hematuria, HTN, oliguria, mild proteinuria (vs nephrotic: massive proteinuria)
Low C3: post-strep GN, MPGN, lupus, endocarditis, shunt nephritis
Normal C3: IgA nephropathy, ANCA vasculitis, anti-GBM
Post-streptococcal GN: 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis, self-limited in children
IgA nephropathy: most common GN worldwide, synpharyngitic hematuria
RPGN (crescentic): medical emergency - anti-GBM, ANCA, immune complex
description: Inflammation of the tiny filters in the kidneys (glomeruli) that can cause blood and protein in the urine, high blood pressure, and kidney damage from various immune-mediated causes.

name: Hyponatremia
signs:
Headache
Nausea/vomiting
Confusion
Lethargy
Seizures (severe, <120)
Muscle cramps
Gait instability
Cerebral edema
Respiratory arrest (severe)
treatment:
1. Assess volume status (hypovolemic vs euvolemic vs hypervolemic)
2. Severe symptomatic: 3% hypertonic saline
3. Rate of correction: max 8-10 mEq/L per 24h (avoid ODS)
4. Hypovolemic: normal saline
5. Euvolemic (SIADH): fluid restriction, salt tabs, vasopressin antagonists
6. Hypervolemic (CHF, cirrhosis): fluid restriction, diuretics
meds:
3% hypertonic saline 100-150mL IV bolus (severe symptoms)
Normal saline (hypovolemic)
Tolvaptan 15-60mg daily (SIADH, cautious monitoring)
Demeclocycline 300-600mg BID (SIADH)
Salt tablets 1-3g TID
Furosemide 20-40mg IV (hypervolemic)
Desmopressin 1-2mcg IV (rescue if overcorrecting)
ddx: SIADH, CHF, Cirrhosis, Hypothyroidism, Adrenal insufficiency, Psychogenic polydipsia, Beer potomania, Diuretic use, Pseudohyponatremia (hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia)
labs:
Serum sodium, serum osmolality
Urine sodium and osmolality (key to differential)
Volume status assessment
BMP
TSH, morning cortisol
Glucose (correct sodium for hyperglycemia: add 1.6 mEq per 100mg/dL glucose >100)
LFTs, BNP if hypervolemic
CT head if neurologic symptoms
notes:
Serum osm <280 = true hyponatremia
SIADH: urine osm >100, urine Na >40, euvolemic
Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS): if sodium corrected too fast (>10-12 mEq/24h)
ODS rescue: desmopressin + D5W to re-lower sodium if overcorrecting
Chronic (>48h): correct slowly. Acute (<48h): can correct faster
Most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalized patients
Beer potomania: low solute intake limits free water excretion
description: An electrolyte imbalance where sodium levels in the blood are abnormally low, which can cause brain swelling and neurological symptoms ranging from mild confusion to seizures and coma.

name: Renal Calculi
signs:
Severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin
Hematuria (microscopic or gross)
Nausea/vomiting
Urinary urgency and frequency
Restlessness (cannot find comfortable position)
CVA tenderness
Fever (if concurrent infection = emergency)
treatment:
1. Pain control: NSAIDs first-line (ketorolac), opioids if needed
2. Medical expulsive therapy: tamsulosin for stones 5-10mm
3. IVF hydration
4. Strain urine for stone analysis
5. Ureteroscopy or lithotripsy for stones >10mm or refractory
6. PCNL for large or staghorn stones
7. Urgent urology if fever + obstruction (obstructive pyelonephritis)
meds:
Ketorolac 30mg IV or 10mg PO q6h
Ibuprofen 600mg PO q8h
Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily (medical expulsive therapy)
Ondansetron 4mg IV/PO PRN
Oxycodone 5mg PO q4h PRN (breakthrough)
HCTZ 25mg daily + potassium citrate (calcium stone prevention)
Allopurinol 100-300mg daily (uric acid stones)
ddx: Pyelonephritis, Appendicitis, Ovarian torsion, Ectopic pregnancy, AAA, Musculoskeletal pain, Renal cell carcinoma
labs:
CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast (gold standard - 95% sensitivity)
Urinalysis (hematuria, crystals, pH)
BMP (creatinine)
CBC (leukocytosis if infected)
Uric acid level
Parathyroid hormone and calcium (hyperparathyroidism)
24h urine collection (after first stone for metabolic evaluation)
Stone analysis when passed
notes:
Types: calcium oxalate (80%), calcium phosphate, uric acid (radiolucent on XR), struvite (infection/staghorn), cystine
Stones <5mm: 90% pass spontaneously. 5-10mm: 50%. >10mm: usually need intervention
Fever + obstructing stone = urological emergency (urgent decompression: stent or nephrostomy)
Prevention: increase fluid intake (>2.5L/day), low sodium, normal calcium diet
Struvite stones: urease-producing bacteria (Proteus, Klebsiella) - require complete surgical removal
Cystine stones: autosomal recessive, hexagonal crystals, alkalinize urine
description: Hard mineral deposits that form in the kidneys and can cause excruciating pain as they travel through the urinary tract, commonly known as kidney stones.

name: Pyelonephritis
signs:
Fever and chills
Flank pain and CVA tenderness
Nausea/vomiting
Dysuria and urinary frequency
Malaise
Abdominal pain
May present as sepsis
treatment:
1. Outpatient: oral fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX if uncomplicated
2. Inpatient: IV antibiotics for severe, complicated, or vomiting
3. Blood cultures if septic
4. CT if no improvement in 48-72h (rule out abscess)
5. Urologic consultation if obstruction
meds:
Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID x7 days (outpatient)
TMP-SMX DS PO BID x14 days (outpatient)
Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily (inpatient)
Piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h (severe)
Gentamicin 5mg/kg IV daily + ampicillin 2g IV q4h
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
ddx: Lower UTI (cystitis), Renal calculi, Appendicitis, Diverticulitis, PID, Musculoskeletal pain, Renal abscess, Renal infarction
labs:
Urinalysis with culture (pyuria, bacteriuria, WBC casts)
Blood cultures x2
CBC (leukocytosis)
BMP
Lactate if sepsis concern
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (if complicated or no improvement at 48-72h)
Renal ultrasound (obstruction)
notes:
E. coli causes 75-95% of uncomplicated cases
Complicated: male, structural abnormality, obstruction, pregnancy, immunocompromised, catheter
Pregnancy: hospitalize, IV ceftriaxone - high risk for preterm labor
Perinephric abscess: suspect if no improvement after 72h antibiotics
Emphysematous pyelonephritis: gas-forming infection in diabetics, often needs nephrectomy
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis: chronic, mimics renal cell carcinoma
description: A bacterial kidney infection that causes flank pain, fever, and urinary symptoms, requiring antibiotic treatment to prevent serious complications like sepsis or kidney damage.
